mechanism_narrative: Rising CPI (CPIAUCSL) erodes the real purchasing power of fixed-income cash flows, compelling investors to demand higher nominal yields as compensation for inflation risk. This effect is persistent and does not require a crossing threshold.
failure_condition: The rolling 12-month correlation between monthly CPIAUCSL changes and 20-day yield changes is less than 0.2, or the average yield change following positive CPI months is not significantly positive at the 5% level.

mechanism_narrative: When the federal funds rate drops below 2%, the Fed is perceived as near the zero lower bound and committed to prolonged accommodation. This triggers a flight-to-safety and aggressive duration buying, compressing Treasury yields.
regime_hint: monetary easing at zero lower bound
failure_condition: In at least 40% of trading days where FEDFUNDS is below 2%, the subsequent 5-day yield change is positive, or the cumulative average 5-day yield change across such windows is not negative.

mechanism_narrative: A persistent two-month concurrent decline in housing starts and retail sales signals a broad demand collapse. This strengthens expectations of aggressive Fed rate cuts and disinflation, reducing 10-year yields over a longer horizon.
candidate_trigger: HOUST month-over-month decline for 2 consecutive months AND RSAFS month-over-month decline for 2 consecutive months
failure_condition: Following the joint decline pattern, the 20-day yield change is positive in more than 30% of instances over the subsequent 60 trading days, or the median 60-day cumulative yield change is not negative.

mechanism_narrative: During a monetary tightening cycle (FEDFUNDS above 4%), rising CPI reinforces expectations of further rate hikes, driving yields higher. In an easing cycle the effect reverses as rising CPI is viewed as transitory and yields fall.
failure_condition: When FEDFUNDS > 4% and CPI rises, the subsequent 20-day yield change is negative in more than 40% of cases. Also, during easing cycles (FEDFUNDS < 2%), the same CPI pattern should lead to negative yield changes in >60% of cases—if not, the regime dependence is falsified.

mechanism_narrative: A sustained decline in industrial production signals weakening economic activity and falling capacity utilization, which reduces inflation pressures and drags down long-term yields via lower breakeven rates.
failure_condition: The rolling 12-month correlation between monthly INDPRO changes and 60-day yield changes is not significantly positive (i.e., the mechanism requires that falling INDPRO coincides with falling yields, correlation > 0.3). If the correlation is below 0.2 over any 3-year rolling window, the hypothesis is rejected.

mechanism_narrative: When the unemployment rate exceeds 5%, it crosses a widely watched recession threshold, triggering flight-to-safety flows and expectations of imminent Fed easing, both of which push Treasury yields lower.
failure_condition: In at least 40% of occurrences where UNRATE exceeds 5%, the forward 20-day yield change is positive, or the average 20-day yield change across such episodes is not significantly negative (t-test p > 0.05).

mechanism_narrative: A simultaneous three-month increase in unemployment and decrease in the federal funds rate reflects a coordinated economic deterioration and monetary policy response. This joint signal reinforces disinflation and safe-haven demand, lowering yields.
candidate_trigger: UNRATE rolling 3-month increase >= 0.3 percentage points AND FEDFUNDS rolling 3-month decrease >= 25 basis points
failure_condition: Following the joint pattern, the 20-day yield change is positive in more than 30% of instances, or the average yield change is not negative at the 5% significance level.

mechanism_narrative: When the unemployment rate is low (<4%), a rise in housing starts signals overheating demand and resource constraints, pushing yields higher. When unemployment is high (>6%), a rise in starts signals recovery and yields fall as recession fears ease.
failure_condition: When UNRATE < 4% and HOUST rises, the subsequent 20-day yield change is negative in more than 40% of cases. Additionally, when UNRATE > 6% and HOUST rises, the yield change should be negative in >60% of cases—if either condition fails, regime dependence is not supported.
